speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you worked in the office for about a month. And you lost some of the swelling. So then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got rich. Because they gave me material for my work, for a dress. They gave me soap, which was very important, and a few kilo of rice. And I came back to my room that I had. And I was a millionaire-- very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went back to the place where you worked at the rice fields?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I had to go back to the kolkhoz.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So in a month you became a millionaire. Wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. That happiness you couldn't buy for millions-- at that time-- that I'm not going to die, and I'm going to see my sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Gordon stayed back at the rice fields.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I got a letter from a friend of mine, which I went to school together. Then my future husband is in town, in Tashkent. And at that time, if you find anybody from the same town, you were anxious to see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you friends before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to school, but we were not friends, because we knew each other. And he was older than I am. But at that time, it didn't mean anything, just anybody from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took a few kilos of rice. I sold them on the market. And I'm a millionaire. And I went to Tashkent. I walked to see my friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she told me that he's there, and this-- I am too. And right away, he wanted to help me. He'll give me money, he'll give me this. I don't need it, because I'm a millionaire. I don't need nothing now. If he would come a month before, maybe I would. And he suggested I should go to-- it was in Siberia, in Chelyabinsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My future husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thought you met him in Tashkent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. He offered me to come to Chelyabinsk. He had to go back to Chelyabinsk, because he came special.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he come from Chelyabinsk to Tashkent?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went, I think, by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but I mean what--

speaker: interviewee
text: They had to, at the end, arrive. At that time you had to have a special, like we call a visa. He got a visa where he is working, then he can go to Tashkent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he come to Tashkent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why he came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe there was a few boys from our town. They knew that I'm alive. But nobody could find me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came to look for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to look for me, and the other boys came back. They couldn't find me. But my husband find me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You found him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I found him. You're right. And I didn't go right away, because I worked on the farm. And I had to take off. For all the years what I worked, the rice, which belonged to me. So I stood there all the time, and I said I'm not going to go until I take off the rice. And he came again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were paid--

speaker: interviewee
text: For the work that we were done, and what we worked for so long. We were paid with rice.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the end of the year, or at the end of the month?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At the end of the two years what I was working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: For all the time. So I wouldn't go without taking off. Because what they did, you had to work for the government all day for them. But if you had parents, or grandparents, they worked. And they gave you a little piece of land, and if you'll do it, after working all day, then belongs to you. I worked with my nephew when it was-- the moon-- and it was light nights. So I was staying on the field at night. And in daytime, we worked for the government. And we worked on this a piece of land, which belonged to us. And then I wouldn't go for nowhere, to take off the rice. And we had, forever, many pounds of rice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did your husband-to-be want you to go to Chelyabinsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he had a good job there. And he thought, maybe it's hard to bring a horse. And he will help me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he's sad that I'm not coming. So in 1943, the 20th of October, he came back to Tashkent. And I left the farm at that time. We got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the 1943, you got married, and you went to Chelyabinsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during all that time, did you know what happened back in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew it, and the family is all gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rumors.

speaker: interviewer
text: From whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: From people, refugees came. We knew then we haven't got nobody there. It's gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear anything from your sisters from Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once I used to write the addresses when I was home. And I hardly remember them. But I decided one day we're going to be hungry. And we'll sell the normal what they would give us. And we'll send a telegram to my sister. And luckily, she got it. And right away, we got back. Then maybe she can help us, and maybe she would, anything she can do. And I wrote back, then we are living good, and we don't need any help. Because I knew then Russia will not let out the letter. So I wrote, never, don't send nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were your sisters? In which city did they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Winnipeg, Manitoba, one sister were in Winnipeg, Manitoba, one Regina, Saskatchewan. And I came in 1947. We came to Winnipeg.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, but I'd like to take you back to 1943 now. So you married your husband. And his name is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Tessler.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Manley, M-A-N-L-E-Y. T-E-S-S-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went with him to Chelyabinsk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was life in Chelyabinsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a good life. But as soon as we came, they wanted to take him to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Right. He was like [NON-ENGLISH] then all the people they couldn't take in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how to say it in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say in another language.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Then they cannot take them to the army. They were working in factories, but not in the army. But then a visit came. Then they want him to the army. So my husband came to the main man, paid him watches and bribed him. And got papers then he's going back to Tashkent. And we left. I wasn't there too long. And we left back to Tashkent.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do now in Tashkent, now you're a married lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a married lady. My husband worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked in a company. He was a chauffeur in Korets.

speaker: interviewer
text: In?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: A chauffeur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Like he could drive a truck, he was a mechanic.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a driver.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a driver, yes. So being in Tashkent, he could get the best job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they didn't have people to drive trucks, and cars, and all the thing. Because most of the people were on the army. So they took him. And again, he got papers. And they cannot take him to the army. And he worked, half for him, half of the government, and made a life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, after three months, I got pregnant. And my oldest daughter was born in 1944. And it was very hard. Even if you got money, you couldn't get anything for the baby. It was very hard to raise a baby at that time. Well, I don't think it should have been that. We couldn't get diapers. We couldn't get anything, powder. I remember we took potatoes, what we supposed to eat, and put it, it should evaporate to get starch for the baby. Diapers, for nine months I washed hard linen. And I was lucky that I could get it on the black market. And I washed it for nine months every single day, until it should get a little bit soft.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the other women do for their babies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very seldom children were born.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was still the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all the men were gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not always, but I don't know. It was an accident, I got pregnant. But then, of course, I wanted an abortion. I came to a doctor. A very nice man, he was like a father. And I had 500 rubles. And I was offering to get an abortion. And he listened to me. And I told him. I'm afraid my husband, they will take him to the army, and I'll be left with the baby. And I cannot support myself, not with a baby. And he said to me, I understand. You'll be very thankful. I said, I sure will. But you're aiming to come to Canada. You're aiming to come to your sisters. And then you'll have a good life. Maybe you wouldn't able to have children. And he talked me out of it. And my daughter was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you had your daughter, did you go to a hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We went to the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what else do you remember from your times in Tashkent? What was the worst thing that happened to you during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The worst thing that happened that I was full for hunger. And I didn't know. And then the doctor told me that I have six months to live. This was the first thing. Because I wasn't afraid to work, and I worked in these factories, and on the field, anything just to survive. But if I haven't got vitamins, and I'm going to die, this is what the thing was. And of course, I knew that I haven't got nobody except my two sisters. And my whole family is gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from the time you communicated by telegram with your sisters, did you then start keeping in touch with one another?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too often, because I was afraid that the letters will not come. But as much as I could, I wrote letters to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Gordon was with you all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got married, when you went to Chelyabinsk, did Gordon go with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he moved back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we returned back.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. What are some things that you remember which are good things, that people did nice things, kind things for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I do remember. Walking home, before I came to Tashkent, we were walking, and then on horses, and on donkeys, just to survive, to go farther. Hitler shouldn't catch us. We went in to a farm. A Russian lady, an older lady, and I asked, I have money. But we are hungry. Maybe she'll give us a piece of bread. She opened anything she had. And she put us bread, and milk, and sour cream, and all the stuff what she had. And we could eat as much as I can. And I asked her, I cannot afford to eat everything, because I haven't got so much money to pay. And she's, never mind. I don't want any money from you. Maybe God will help me. Then somebody-- she had a son in the army. And she said, maybe somebody will feel sorry for my son. This was once. Then we walked from Tashkent to get winter job from the kolkhoz to Tashkent, maybe they can get a job in Tashkent. Because it was cold. We couldn't work on the rice no more. And it was the first year what they had snow. And we walked and walked. And I didn't have the shoes. I put on-- they were torn already. And I walked on the ice and the snow. And I begged Gordon. Leave me. I'll die. I cannot do it. But you go farther. Maybe you'll find. And from far we saw a little bit of-- from the chimney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Smoke.

speaker: interviewee
text: The smoke. And he said, well, just try your best, little by little. Maybe we'll go into this little house. And I don't know how, but we did it. And as soon as I opened the door, I fainted. And there was a little tiny room, two girls were sleeping there. And there was a little bit of fire in the oven. An old lady opened the door. And she saw that I'm frozen. She didn't ask if I'm sick, or if I'm with lice, running around like that. She picked me up, and put me in between the two girls to warm me up, and brought me back to life. She didn't have nothing in the house, but she had only hot water. And the girls had to go to work in the morning. Very, very poor. She gave me hot water to drink. And I'll never forget her. She saved my life. That's the good things.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So we're back in Tashkent.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, and we were there till the war finished, and then in different places the war still. But the Russians said, that they're letting out the people from the Ukraine, [NON-ENGLISH] Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: The western Ukraine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then we can go back where we were born. And the baby was a few months old. And I was afraid then today they let you out. And the more they can change their mind. So we took the baby. And we went to Lwow, which was still Russia. And there we were a few months in Russia. Coming to Poland we went, it was 40 below. The snow was very high. We went with a open train, like for cattle train. And we stopped before Krakow. And we were staying there maybe a week time, because the road was very bad. And we couldn't go further. The only miracle was then I nursed the baby. But after so many days, and they gave us-- we didn't have any water. And food they gave us very little. They didn't have at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you food?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the train. On the train we had food.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this was your own private trip, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: But at that time, you don't pay for nothing. We didn't pay to go on the train. Everything was free, if you could survive, if you could go in. And if not-- I remember the train was running already before even I got married, and Gordon was on the train. And I run. Then the train was already going. And I kept outside. And I kept my hands outside on the two bars, so tight. Because I knew I'm going to get killed until they open the door on and let me in. And they want to take my hands apart. It took them a long time, because there were floors. So at that time you could go not on your own. You didn't have to pay, as long as you could go in. Everything was full. So we didn't pay for nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who provided food on these trains? The army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. I believe then we had food with us. But we didn't have any water. Many things I tried to block out of my mind to be able to sleep at night. Because if I'll remember everything, I wouldn't be able to sleep at night. So many things I try not to remember, which is not very important. How I got the food, I don't even remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you went to Lwow, and then to Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Before Krakow when I fainted. I fainted in Krakow too, because it was very, very cold. And I hold the baby. And we came in to any house, or whatever it was left, just they should let us there on the floor, just to warm up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I made it. And we were, I don't know, which house it was, or a building. And I came to myself back. But we weren't too long. I wanted to run out from Poland, from Russia, to get any country, just closer to Canada. My aim, I wouldn't give up. I have to go to Canada. And that Gordon came with you, and your husband, and the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. So we got papers. Then we going. We're Romanian people, we're going to Romania. Whether it be Romania, or Chile, I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were false papers, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You bought them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if we bought. We didn't have money to buy it. It was, I think, the organization's. I don't remember even, if we bought, or organization, we got papers. Then we going to Romania. Going to Romania, we stopped in Prague, just to go out, to have a drink, or to have a rest. Melly has the sister, which she survived too, and she was in Prague. We arranged with her, and we left everything on the train, and we went out there. The car was ready. We went into the car. And we disappeared. We didn't go to Romania. In Prague, we were there for two years, till 1947, until my sister sent us papers. At that time, the immigration was still closed. But my sister paid, and with all the things, got us as farmers. We never saw the farm. But we were the first one to come to Canada, the way she promised. And this was in 1947, November the 5th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you land in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Winnipeg.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. My sister had ready apartment. For six months she paid until we'll come. And we had apartment. Because at that time they never let in with children. And the apartment was close to my sister. And we were there three years. Then it was a flood, in Winnipeg, flood. And Reesa was, my second daughter, was born. And she was only a few months. And we had to leave the house, because the house was half in water. And we went to Regina, to my sister. And then they moved out of Regina. And in Regina, we were from 1950 to 1959, I believe. And then we came to Toronto. It wasn't so easy, but it was nice then we were in a free country. And if you want to work, nobody stops you. We worked very hard, all different kinds of jobs. And my husband, we had a little store in Winnipeg with yogurt. Then we came to Regina. My husband had a store, a kosher store, with meat. He was never a butcher. And we bought houses. And I cleaned and cooked and washed for 20 people. We sold the house, bought houses. I took out, bought old broken house, fixed them. We threw out from the garbage, trucks, from the garbage, from the basement, and make money. My husband went on the farm. I was with three children, with a 15 room house, cleaning, and cooking, and washing, with the three children. And he was on the farm buying cattle. And until he start with his back. He pulled a cow, and ruined his back, pulled a cow on a truck, and ruined his back. So he couldn't go no more on the farm. In the meantime, my children, my daughter was 14 and 1/2 already. There was not too many families in Regina, so each child went to a different school. And I saw it's no good. The children what they are in my house, and sleeping with my child and eating. And when she got married she told her, you are dirty Jew. So I thought, we have to move. And we moved to Toronto. I had family in Toronto, cousins. And we're still in Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you say was life for you in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hard, but good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hard but the good. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We made a success.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sonia, why did you decide to do this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my children and my grandchildren know very well. I talked to them. I told them all my stories, and they know it. But I decided, let this be for generations which the people, they hide for their own children. They never want to talk to them. They thought with this thing, they'll save them all the problems what they went through in life. I think it was wrong. And my children was after me to make the tape too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, then. Is there anything else you would like to add before we finish?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you ask me, I'll tell you. But otherwise, I'm a little bit, shall I say, nervous.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your experience and the war change your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: It changed that nothing is hard for me to do, nothing. I worked 40 hours a day. I was thinking then when my children will grow up, they'll never know who is the father. Because when he got up, 4 o'clock, they were sleeping. When he came home, 12 o'clock, they were sleeping. And my children will grow up with not knowing who the father is. But as it happened, the beginning was very hard. And we worked until today. And we have a nice home, nice grandchildren, 10 grandchildren, 3 great grandchildren. And I'm very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This is my husband Manley Tessler. We are married 52 years. That's all. And it will be-- some other guys introduced the same thing. You'll do it. This is my family. From left to right, the upper one is my oldest sister, Ruchel, and the husband, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is he? Where is [NON-ENGLISH] on the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left. From the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: In front of your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: In front of my sister is her husband. And the baby, I believe that's Gordon. The second from left is my sister, Tzipora. She is in Israel. And the third one is my sister, Silvia. This was the year she went to Canada. My sister Devora, near her is her husband, and the two babies. And there, another child, there were three boys and they were all killed. The second row is my father, Avram, my mother, Rivka, and my brother, Yacov. The last row is my nephew, Gordon. That's me, and my sister, Chava. I believe that's it. Except there were more children, but they got killed. This is my two sisters when they left in the late '20s to Canada. The first one, her name is Esther. And the second is Silvia. This was me. I was 16 at the time, taken in Korets, at home. On the bottom of this picture is my daughter, Tzilia and her husband, Harvey, the granddaughter, Lisa, her husband, Marty, and the grandchildren, Rachel, and Ryan, and her son, my grandson, Danny, his wife, Maggie, and the baby, the great grandchild, Justin. From left, there is my granddaughter, Lori, my daughter Reesa, my grandson, Avi, which belongs to Debbie, my second daughter, Ari, my grandson, Kerry, my granddaughter, Sury, my granddaughter, and David my son-in-law. This is my grandson, Zev, my daughter, Debbie, my granddaughter, Cheri, my grandson, Barry, my grandson Avi, and my son-in-law, Ed. That's it.
